title: while Loops
subtitle: Repeat code as long as a condition stays True
learning_objectives: Write a while loop that repeats until a condition becomes False
Use a counter variable to control how many times a loop runs
Explain why infinite loops are dangerous and how to avoid them
Use while True with break to exit a loop from inside

sections.heading: while Loop Syntax
sections.content:
A while loop checks its condition before each iteration. As long as the condition is True, the indented block runs again. When the condition becomes False, the loop stops and execution continues after the loop.

The most common pattern is to use a counter variable that you update inside the loop so the condition eventually becomes False.
sections.code_example:
count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print("Count:", count)
    count = count + 1

print("Loop finished!")
sections.code_output:
Count: 1
Count: 2
Count: 3
Count: 4
Count: 5
Loop finished!

sections.heading: Infinite Loops: A Dangerous Mistake
sections.content:
An infinite loop runs forever because its condition never becomes False. This will freeze your browser tab and force you to refresh the page, losing any unsaved work.

The most common cause is forgetting to update the loop variable inside the loop. Always ask yourself: what will make this condition False eventually?
sections.warning: Infinite loops will freeze your browser! Always make sure your loop condition will eventually become False.
sections.code_example:
# DANGER: This loop never ends — do NOT run this!
# count = 1
# while count <= 5:
#     print(count)
#     # Forgot to update count! count stays 1 forever.

# SAFE version: always update the counter
count = 1
while count <= 3:
    print(count)
    count += 1   # += 1 is shorthand for count = count + 1

sections.heading: while True and break
sections.content:
Sometimes you want to keep looping until a specific event happens inside the loop. The pattern while True: creates a loop that would run forever on its own, but a break statement immediately exits the loop when you're ready.

This is useful when you don't know in advance how many iterations you need.
sections.code_example:
number = 1

while True:
    print("Number:", number)
    number += 1
    if number > 4:
        break

print("Done.")

exercises.title: Countdown
exercises.prompt: Write a while loop that counts down from 5 to 1, printing each number. After the loop, print "Blast off!" Note: Don't forget to decrease count inside the loop (e.g. count -= 1), otherwise the loop will run forever and freeze the page.
exercises.starter_code:
count = 5

# Write your while loop below
exercises.solution:
count = 5

while count >= 1:
    print(count)
    count -= 1

print("Blast off!")
hints: The loop should run while count >= 1
Use count -= 1 to decrease the counter each iteration

exercises.title: Sum Accumulator
exercises.prompt: Use a while loop to calculate the sum of numbers from 1 to 10. Start with total = 0 and n = 1. Add n to total in each iteration. Print the final total after the loop. Note: Remember to increment n (n += 1) inside the loop, or it will run forever.
exercises.starter_code:
total = 0
n = 1

# Write your while loop below

# Print the total after the loop
exercises.solution:
total = 0
n = 1

while n <= 10:
    total += n
    n += 1

print("Total:", total)
hints: Inside the loop: total += n, then n += 1

tests.message: The sum of 1 to 10 should be 55

quiz.question: What causes an infinite loop in a while statement?
quiz.explanation: An infinite loop occurs when the while condition never evaluates to False. This usually happens when the loop variable is never updated, so the condition remains True forever.

quiz.question: What does count += 1 mean?
options: Add count and 1 without saving the result
Increase count by 1 (same as count = count + 1)
quiz.explanation: The += operator is shorthand for addition-assignment. count += 1 is exactly the same as count = count + 1 — it increases count by 1 and stores the result back in count.

completion_message: Fantastic! You now know how to use while loops to repeat code, manage counter variables, and avoid the danger of infinite loops. Next up: the for loop, which is even more common in Python!
